fromgoldencombsguide-text:
"How doth the little busy bee
Improve each shining hour
and gather honey all the day
From every opening flower!"
 - Isaac Watts

From Golden Combs is a mod that enhances the apiculture ("Beekeeping") opportunities available in Vintage Story. It achieves this by introducing two new types of hive that you can build, the ceramic hive, and the Langstroth hive. Each of these hives have their own benefits and provide enhanced honeycomb production over the skep. Be aware that From Golden Combs hives don't propagate in the manner of the vanilla skep. This was done to ensure that the basic skep remained useful.

The history of apiculture can be traced back at least 10,000 years, which also marks the oldest known depiction of wild hive collection. Many approaches to creating domestic hives were tried, with pottery-based hives appearing about 9,000 years ago. This is also where your journey into advanced apiculture will begin in Vintage Story.

Ceramic hives are created from two components, the first being the ceramic hive. The ceramic hive will be the new home of your bee colony.

The second component is the honey pot, which sits atop the ceramic hive and will be where your colony stores their honey. Together, they create a perpetual source of honey and wax that doesn't require breaking the hive to obtain your honeycomb.

Creation of a ceramic hive starts with the gathering of clay. Any type clay can be used to create the two parts of the ceramic hive using the clayforming process.

You will produce a raw brood pot, and a raw honey pot, and will fire them in a pit kiln. Once they have finished firing, they'll be ready to use!

Now that you have your ceramic hive and honey pot, place your hive wherever you want it. Like skeps, they will require nearby flowers to produce their honey. In addition, you must have an empty honey pot placed on top of the hive for them to fill.

Of course, without bees, there won't be any honey. To fill your hive with bees you'll need a populated skep obtained through the usual manner.

However, instead of placing the populated skep itself, you'll directly interact with the ceramic hive with a populated skep. This will place your bee colony into the hive and consume the skep.

Congratulations! Your ceramic beehive will now produce honey for you! You can tell it's ready to harvest when a yellow ring appears on the honey pot on top of the brood pot.

Harvesting the honey pot is simple. Take it off, place it on the ground, and use a knife on it. You'll receive honeycomb and the honey pot will be empty. Place it back on the ceramic hive, and you're back in business. In addition to being more convenient than skeps, on average they also produce more honeycomb.
fromgoldencombs:fromgoldencombspollinati:
"The pedigree of honey
Does not concern the bee;
A clover, any time, to him
Is aristocracy."
- Emily Dickinson

Bees are one of the most important pollinators of plants in our world. Hives in From Golden Combs are no different. Hives that are at decent or large population will generate "crop charges" over time, up to their maximum. When a nearby crop, berry, or fruit tree is broken the hive will expend a charge and increase the drops from the affected plant. The exact amount of the increase is dependent on the kind of hive providing the boost. Skeps provide the least at 10%, Ceramic Hives provide a boost of 20%, and Langstroth Hives will produce a boost of 30%.
fromgoldencombs:langstrothguide-text:
The crown jewel of any beekeeper is the Langstroth hive. It's a hive that meets the needs of the swarm and the apiculturist alike. It consists of a base, one or more supers (you can have up to three on a base) filled with up to 10 lined frames each, and topped with a populated brood box.. Once settled in, your bees will produce the most honey and wax of all the hives, and will provide the best boost to your crop.

Step 1 - Gathering Your Materials

The Langstroth hive is not a beginners hive, it requires acquiring materials from your fields, an investment wax from your current hives, and wooden boards to build your first one. The efforts will pay you back with abundant crops, bountiful berry bushes, and flourishing fruit trees.

The materials you'll need are:
- 10 Wax (32 for panels, 1 for waxing linen for 5 frame liners for bee frames)
- 27 Boards
- 4 logs of the same type (for the base)
- One Hammer
- 1 Linen (For turning into 5 frame liners, supers can hold 10 each but this will get you started)
- 0.5 liter of pitch glue for making bee frames (resin can be used as well, but is less efficient)
- 15 sticks
- 1 populated skep

Using these materials you will create:
2 Langstroth Superstructures (One will become your brood box.)
1 Langstroth Broodbox Top
1 Langstroth Base (The foundation of your Langstroth Hive)
1 Frame rack for lining, repairing, and harvesting your frames.
5 bee frames (For your bees to fill with comb and honey)
5 frame liners (for lining your frames)

Constructing Your Hive - Start With The Panels
Every part of your Langstroth Hive is going to be constructed using Langstroth Panels. These are just boards that have been soaked in wax in a barrel. 1 wax will turn 4 boards into four panels, and you'll need a total of 27 boards to get yourself started.

You're also going to need to line some bee frames while you're at it, so while you're soaking those boards throw a piece of linen into a barrel with one piece of wax. This will create waxed linen, which you can convert into five frame liners with a knife.

The Rack And The Frames Come Next

You'll want to make your frame rack and bee frames next, since you'll need them to make your hive useful. Once you've constructed your frame rack and bee frames, place the empty ones in the frame rack. Put one liner in each by right-clicking with the liner in your hand. You'll also use this for harvesting your frames and repairing them, but we'll come back to that later.

A Place For Your Bees To Live
Now it's time to build your hive proper! You'll start by building your base, two supers, and a brood box. top. While you're making two supers, one is going to be converted into a Brood Box to hold your swarm.

Once you have your two supers built, you'll place one on the ground. Take the brood box. top you made, and use it on one to turn it into a Broodbox. It's pattern will match that of the super you're making it from. You can use a populated skep on it now to introduce your swarm, or you can wait until your hive is assembled, the choice is yours.

Be aware that once the swarm is in the brood box., it will only fit in a bag slot, not your inventory slot!

And A Place For Them To Work

Now place the other super on the ground and right click it's top. The top will slide back and reveal the compartment where you can place your lined bee frames. You can place the five lined frames inside by rightclicking it with a frame. You'll notice there's extra space for five more. Each super can hold 10 frames, but you can put in any number it will hold and your bees will be happy.

One note: Supers will not accept empty frames as they cannot be used to produce comb. You can store harvestable frames in the hive, but only lined frames will fill with more comb.

Putting It All Together

Now close your super by right clicking its front, then shift-right-click with an empty slot to pick it up. You can now place it on your base by right clicking it with the super. Then just pick up your broodbox and put it on top. Provided that you've got enough flowers around the brood box. has been populated using the skep mentioned earlier, you're in business!

Harvesting Your Hive

Unlike the Ceramic Brood Pot and Skep, the Langstroth Hive doesn't require you to babysit your hive to ensure it continues producing. While the ceramic hive and skep can only produce one harvest each before you have to reset them (by destroying the skep or clearing the honey pot), the Langstroth hive can go for multiple cycles before it's full. Even with a single full super, it will take between 3 and 5 weeks to fill every frame.

When you're ready to harvest them, you simply take your hive apart by removing the broodbox and your super. Open the top, take out your harvestable frames, and take them to the frame rack. Place them in it, and use a knife on them to harvest your comb. lined frames only last so long, and every harvest will do some damage to the frames. If they run out of durability, they'll turn back into lined frames You can repair them by clicking the damaged frame with waxed flax twine.

Put the lined frames back in your super, put it back on the stack, and place your brood box. and you're ready for the next bounty!
fromgoldencombs:block-handbooktext-ceram:
This is the foundation of a hive made from clay pots, and can only be carried in an empty bag slot. Interact with it using a populated skep to fill it with a bee colony, and then place a honey pot on top of it.

Egyptians first kept bees in clay pots made from the Nile River mud, and would even ferry the hives downriver to fertilize a wide variety of crops.
fromgoldencombs:block-handbooktext-hivet: This pot is placed atop the brood pot. If the hive is active and there are enough flowers, it will eventually form a wax ring, indicating that it's time to harvest! Take it off, place it on the ground, and scrape it with a knife to remove the honeycomb. Place it back on the hive, and it will be ready for your bees to fill again!
fromgoldencombs:block-handbooktext-frame: The frame rack is a covenient tool for holding frames that need to be scraped clean, repaired, or filled with a frame liner. Place the frame in the rack, use waxed twine or waxed linen to repair it. You can use a knife to remove honeycomb so you can replace it in the hive.
fromgoldencombs:block-handbooktitle-lang: Langstroth Hive Base - Held in Backpack Slots Only!
fromgoldencombs:block-handbooktext-langs:
Please Be Aware That The Base Of The Langstroth Hive Can Be Made From Any Combination Of Logs And Langstroth Panels.
The Resulting Piece Will Match The Colors Of The Panels Used To Make The Block Or Item.
The Limited Options In The Handbook Are To Keep It Clean OF Countless Variations.
Consult Your Creative Menu To View Other Combination Options You Can Make In Survival.

This is one of the three block components required to make a Langstroth hive. As the name implies it needs to be at the bottom of the stack. You can add a Langstroth Super by it. Top it off with a brood box.
fromgoldencombs:block-handbooktitle-lang: Langstroth Brood Box - Held in Backpack Slots Only!
fromgoldencombs:block-handbooktext-langs:
*
This is the part of your Langstroth Hive that holds your colony of bees. It is created by with a Langstroth Super with no bee frames in it with a brood box top of the same combination of colors. Once created, you place a colony in it by it with a populated skep, this can only be done with the brood box on the ground. Then place it on top of a Langstroth Hive composed of a base and at least one super.
fromgoldencombs:block-handbooktitle-lang: Lagnstroth Superstructure - Held in Backpack Slots Only!
fromgoldencombs:block-handbooktext-langs:
This is where you'll place your bee frames. for your bees to fill. It can made in any combination of two colors of langstroth panels to create a distinct appearance for your hive.
You can open and close it while on the ground by it while highlighting a hotbar slot, or pick it up (if closed) while highlighting an empty bag slot (Ctrl+1-4). When it's open you can fill it with up to 10 lined frames.
When placed in a complete hive your bees will, over time, turn these bee frames into harvestable frames. A complete hive consists of a base, at least one super (with at least one lined frame), and topped by a brood box..
fromgoldencombs:beeswaitfordayswarmthtof: The bees huddle against the cold, waiting for the days warmth to fly.
item-handbooktext-langstrothbroodtop: Use this to create a brood box by an empty super with the same wood pattern. These can be made from the combination of any two wood types of bee frames. It can be made out of anyh combination of two colors of Langstroth panels.
item-handbooktext-langstrothside: A component used in making super, base, frame rack, and brood box top in Langstroth Hives.
item-handbooktext-emptybeeframe: The foundation that your bees will build their comb on. It's not ready yet though! You need to place it in a frame rack and then right-click it with a frame liner to create a lined frame.
item-handbooktext-linedbeeframe: This is a frame ready to be placed into a super for your bees to build comb on. When it's ready to be harvested you just take it out, place it in a frame rack and right-click it with a knife to collect honeycomb. This will also damage the frame. You can repair it using waxed twine in a frame rack. If you allow it to break completely, it will become an empty frame. You can then reline it with a frame liner.
item-handbooktext-harvestablebeeframe: This frame is ready to harvest! Just place it in a frame rack and scrape off the honey with a knife to get honeycomb. You'll damage the frame a little doing it, but they can be repaired with waxed twine in a frame rack. If the frame breaks, you can replace the frame liner.
item-handbooktext-frameliner: Frame liners are created using waxed linen, and a knife. They are then used to line empty frames in a frame rack.
item-handbooktext-waxedflaxtwine: This twine is used for repairing lined frame in a frame rack.
fromgoldencombs:nonemptysuper: The super must be empty to convert to a brood box.
fromgoldencombs:noemptyframes: Unlined frames will not produce honey.
